panels.description: Time since the slurmctld container last (re)started (`container_start_time_seconds`). Container restarts due to crashes or rollouts reset this to zero — useful for spotting unexpected slurmctld restarts.
panels.title: Container Uptime

panels.description: Current pod phase from kube_pod_status_phase.
panels.title: Pod Phase

panels.description: Cumulative container restart count over time. Each step up marks a restart event; flat means none. Drag to zoom into a window when investigating a specific restart.
panels.title: Container Restarts

panels.description: Number of times the controller pod was rescheduled to a different node in the last 24h. Detected via changes in kube_pod_info node label.
panels.title: Reschedules (24h)

panels.description: Number of server threads running inside slurmctld (slurm_controller_server_thread_count).
panels.title: slurmctld Server Threads

panels.description:
Slurmctld container memory working set as % of the container memory limit (`container_memory_working_set_bytes` ÷ `kube_pod_container_resource_limits{resource="memory"}`). Approaching 100% means slurmctld is close to being OOM-killed by the kubelet. Requires a memory limit to be set on the slurmctld container; if no limit is configured this panel will show "No data". For the absolute byte timeseries see the **Memory** row → 'Memory Working Set'.

Value shown is the **1h moving average**, not the instantaneous reading — smooths out scrape jitter and short bursts.

targets.expr: avg_over_time((100 * sum(container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="soperator", pod="$pod", container="slurmctld"}) / sum(kube_pod_container_resource_limits{namespace="soperator", pod="$pod", container="slurmctld", resource="memory"}))[1h:])

panels.title: Memory (1h avg)

panels.description:
Inode usage % of the controller spool PVC (`controller-spool-$pod`) — slurmctld's state-save directory accumulates many small files, so inode pressure typically hits before byte capacity. For bytes usage and the underlying timeseries see the **Storage** row.

Value shown is the **1h moving average**, not the instantaneous reading — smooths out scrape jitter and short bursts.
panels.title: Spool PVC Usage (1h avg)

panels.description:
Slurmctld container CPU usage in cores (5m rate of `container_cpu_usage_seconds_total`). Full CPU timeseries lives in the **System** row → "CPU Usage (cores)" panel.

Value shown is the **1h moving average**, not the instantaneous reading — smooths out scrape jitter and short bursts.
panels.title: CPU Cores (1h avg)

panels.description:
Share of GPUs Slurm is currently using, as reported by the DCGM exporter via the `hpc_job` label (set by soperator's `map_job_dcgm.sh` prolog/epilog).

Both numerator and denominator are joined with `slurm_node_info{is_unavailable=""}` on `exported_pod` ↔ `node_name`, so only GPUs on *available* nodes (not DOWN or DRAINED) are counted. This means drained/down hosts don't dilute the score on either side — no manual ratio correction needed.

Requires the DCGM exporter and soperator's GPU-mapping scripts (default in the chart). CPU-only clusters or non-soperator deployments will show "No data".

Value shown is the **1h moving average**, not the instantaneous reading — smooths out scrape jitter and short bursts.

**Note on join keys**: DCGM's `pod` label is the DaemonSet-pod name and conflicts with the Prometheus scrape target's `pod`, so the scrape rules relabel the original (worker pod) to `exported_pod`. That's the label we join on.

targets.expr: avg_over_time((100 * (count(DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_TEMP{hpc_job!=""} and on(exported_pod) label_replace(slurm_node_info{is_unavailable=""}, "exported_pod", "$1", "node_name", "(.*)")) or vector(0)) / count(DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_TEMP and on(exported_pod) label_replace(slurm_node_info{is_unavailable=""}, "exported_pod", "$1", "node_name", "(.*)")))[1h:])

panels.title: GPU Allocation (1h avg)

panels.description:
Share of GPUs currently doing real work — DCGM reports `DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL ≥ 10%`. Same metric and definition as the **Cluster Usage → GPU Utilization %** timeseries, shown here as an at-a-glance gauge.

Requires the DCGM exporter (default in the soperator chart). CPU-only clusters or non-soperator deployments will show "No data".

Value shown is the **1h moving average**, not the instantaneous reading — smooths out scrape jitter and short bursts.
panels.title: GPU Utilization (1h avg)

panels.description: Container filesystem read/write throughput (rate over 5m). Read positive, Write negative. Split by `device` so per-disk hot spots are visible (typically only one device on the controller pod's spool PVC).
panels.title: Container Filesystem IO

panels.description: Bytes used vs total capacity of the controller-spool PVC over time.
panels.title: Spool PVC Used vs Capacity

panels.description: Used inodes on the controller-spool PVC over time, with total inode capacity as a dashed reference. A steady upward drift hints at an inode leak.
panels.title: Spool PVC Inodes Used

panels.description: Average per-IO wait time on the controller-host's block devices. High values indicate the underlying storage is the bottleneck (relevant for slurmctld spool writes).
panels.title: Disk Wait Time

panels.description: Open file descriptors on the controller node (system-wide). slurmctld can run out of FDs under heavy connection load. Max is omitted when the kernel reports it as effectively unlimited.
panels.title: File Descriptors

panels.description: Per-mountpoint read-only and device-error flags (1 = bad). All zero in healthy state — any non-zero series indicates a filesystem in trouble. Mountpoints that are read-only by design (e.g. /mnt/cloud-metadata) are excluded.
panels.title: Filesystem ReadOnly / Error

panels.description: Per-device read and write IOPS on the controller node.
panels.title: Disk IOps

panels.description: Per-device read/write throughput at the block-device layer. Differs from Container Filesystem IO (which is cgroup-scoped) — surfaces noisy-neighbour contention if any.
panels.title: Disk I/O Throughput

panels.description: Fraction of time each block device was busy serving I/O. Persistent values near 1 mean the device is the bottleneck.
panels.title: Disk I/O Utilization

panels.description: Jobs in any non-terminal state (`PENDING`, `RUNNING`, `SUSPENDED`, `COMPLETING`, `CONFIGURING`, `RESIZING`, `REQUEUED`, `SIGNALING`, `STAGE_OUT`), sourced from the soperator exporter (`slurm_job_info`). Stacked — the total is the count of jobs slurmctld is currently tracking. PENDING is the most operationally significant: a sustained pending backlog degrades scheduler performance (more jobs per cycle, higher chance of hitting `default_queue_depth` / `bf_max_job_test`). Cross-check with the Main Scheduler exit-reasons panel when PENDING climbs.
panels.title: Active Jobs

panels.description:
Share of GPUs Slurm is currently using, per-scrape. Both numerator and denominator are joined with `slurm_node_info{is_unavailable=""}` on `exported_pod` ↔ `node_name`, so only GPUs on *available* nodes (not DOWN or DRAINED) are counted — drained/down hosts don't dilute the score on either side.

Complement to the GPU Utilization % panel — together they show scheduler packing vs actual GPU work.

**Note on join keys**: DCGM's `pod` label is the DaemonSet-pod name and conflicts with the Prometheus scrape target's `pod`, so the scrape rules relabel the original (worker pod) to `exported_pod`. That's the label we join on.

targets.expr: 100 * (count(DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_TEMP{hpc_job!=""} and on(exported_pod) label_replace(slurm_node_info{is_unavailable=""}, "exported_pod", "$1", "node_name", "(.*)")) or vector(0)) / count(DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_TEMP and on(exported_pod) label_replace(slurm_node_info{is_unavailable=""}, "exported_pod", "$1", "node_name", "(.*)"))

panels.title: GPU Allocation %

panels.description:
Share of GPUs currently doing real work (DCGM reports `DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL ≥ 10%`). Distinct from allocation: a GPU can be allocated to a Slurm job but idle (the job isn't actively using it). Mirrors the 'Cluster Utilization' panel in the Slurm Cluster Health dashboard.

This value should generally stay **below** GPU allocation %. The gap between allocation and utilization is the cost of running real jobs — GPUs assigned but not computing yet (container startup, dataset load, IO stalls, gradient sync waits, idle launchers, multi-node sync barriers). A widening gap means jobs are spending more time waiting than computing.
panels.title: GPU Utilization %

panels.description:
Node states breakdown:
- **Down**: Nodes not operational (DOWN or ERROR states)
- **Drained**: IDLE nodes with DRAIN flag
- **Draining**: Nodes with DRAIN flag running jobs
- **Busy**: Nodes running jobs normally (ALLOCATED or MIXED)
- **Idle**: Nodes ready and available to accept new jobs
- **Maintenance**: Nodes temporarily unavailable for administrative tasks
panels.title: Nodes by State

panels.description:
Share of *schedulable* nodes Slurm is currently using (at least partially). Numerator: nodes where `state_base ∈ {ALLOCATED, MIXED}` regardless of drain flag — DRAINING nodes are still doing useful work and count. Denominator: `count(slurm_node_info{is_unavailable=""})` — the exporter's computed schedulability flag (`true` when node is DOWN+* or IDLE+DRAIN+*, empty otherwise). So DOWN and DRAINED nodes are excluded from both sides automatically.

Complement to the **GPU Allocation %** panel above: node-level packing is coarser but works on CPU-only clusters and doesn't depend on DCGM.
panels.title: Node Allocation %

panels.description: RPC call rate broken down by message type (rate over 5m, stacked).
panels.title: RPC Rate per Message Type

panels.description: Average RPC latency per message type (rate(duration)/rate(calls), 5m window).
panels.title: RPC Latency per Message Type

panels.description:
Per-message-type RPC processing time, as `rate(duration_seconds[5m])` without dividing by call count. Stacked area; the total stack height is the cluster-wide RPC processing concurrency.

Reading: a value of 1.0 for a message type means one worker thread of slurmctld was on average kept fully busy by that message type over the last 5 minutes. Latency × call rate = duration rate — this panel highlights the message types that *cost* the most slurmctld time, even if individual calls are cheap.
panels.title: RPC Total Duration per Message Type

panels.description: Top 10 users by RPC call rate (rate of slurm_controller_rpc_user_calls_total over 5m).
panels.title: Top Users by RPC Rate

panels.description: Top 10 users by aggregate RPC time consumed per second (rate of slurm_controller_rpc_user_duration_seconds_total over 5m). A value of 1 means a user kept slurmctld busy for one full thread-second every second on average.
panels.title: Top Users by RPC Total Duration

panels.description: Top 10 users by average RPC latency (rate of `slurm_controller_rpc_user_duration_seconds_total` divided by rate of `slurm_controller_rpc_user_calls_total`, 5m window). High latency for a specific user often points at expensive RPC patterns (`squeue` with large filters, repeated `scontrol show job` polls, etc.).
panels.title: Top Users by RPC Latency

panels.description: slurmctld → slurmd outbound RPC pool. `queued` is messages waiting to dispatch; should sit near zero. Sustained non-zero queue means dispatch is slower than enqueue and downstream slurmd operations (job start/cancel/reconfig) are being delayed.
panels.title: Outbound RPC (Agent) Queue

panels.description: Number of accounting messages slurmctld has buffered for slurmdbd. Should be near zero; growth means slurmdbd is unreachable or slow. Sustained growth is followed by a flush storm when slurmdbd recovers.
panels.title: slurmdbd Queue

panels.description: Duration of the most recent main scheduler cycle. The slurmctld native endpoint exposes only the *last* cycle, so each scrape captures one sample (default scrape interval 30s) — cycles that ran between scrapes are not represented. Yellow above 50 ms, red above 500 ms.
panels.title: Cycle Duration

panels.description: Main scheduler throughput and the last-cycle queue length (both from the slurmctld native OpenMetrics endpoint, Slurm 25.x+). For the exporter-based pending-jobs view, see the Cluster Usage row.
panels.title: Cycles / Min & Queue Length

panels.description:
Rate of main scheduling cycles that ended for each reason — same data as `sdiag` → 'Main scheduling statistics' → 'Last cycle exited as'. The healthy series is `End of job queue`; every other reason means the cycle was cut short by a cap.

- `End of job queue` (`slurm_sched_exit_end`) — examined every pending job and finished. Desired.
- `Hit default_queue_depth` (`slurm_sched_exit_max_depth`) — capped after examining `default_queue_depth` jobs (SchedulerParameters, default 100). Raise if consistently non-zero.
- `Hit sched_max_job_start` (`slurm_sched_exit_max_job_start`) — started `sched_max_job_start` jobs in one cycle (default 0 = unlimited; non-zero only if explicitly set).
- `Blocked on licenses` (`slurm_sched_exit_lic`) — pending jobs were waiting on Slurm licenses; check `sacctmgr show license`.
- `Hit max_rpc_cnt` (`slurm_sched_exit_rpc_cnt`) — slurmctld's RPC queue grew past `max_rpc_cnt`; scheduler deferred to drain it.
- `Timeout (max_sched_time)` (`slurm_sched_exit_timeout`) — cycle exceeded `max_sched_time` seconds and was cut short.

A non-zero rate on any reason other than `End of job queue` means the main scheduler is being throttled — raise the matching cap in `SchedulerParameters`.
panels.title: Main Scheduler Exit Reasons

panels.description: Whether the backfill scheduler is currently mid-cycle (`slurm_bf_active`: 0 = idle, 1 = running).
panels.title: Backfill Active

panels.description: Wall-clock age of the most recent backfill cycle (`time() - slurm_bf_when_last_cycle`). Sawtooth pattern; spikes indicate stalls. Yellow over 1 min, red over 5 min — likely a hung backfill.
panels.title: Time Since Last Cycle

panels.description: Duration of the most recent backfill cycle. The slurmctld native endpoint exposes only the *last* cycle, so each scrape captures one sample (default scrape interval 30s) — cycles that ran between scrapes are not represented. Backfill is naturally slower than the main scheduler; yellow above 1 s, red above 5 s.
panels.title: Last Cycle Duration

panels.description: Backfill cycles per minute (5m rate × 60). Rate is bounded above by `1 / bf_interval` (default 30s → 2/min).
panels.title: Cycles / Min

panels.description: Pending jobs awaiting backfill processing.
panels.title: Backfill Queue Length

panels.description: `all` = jobs examined; `try` = jobs the scheduler actually attempted to start (the CPU-intensive subset).
panels.title: Depth (Jobs Examined Per Cycle)

panels.description: Distinct reservation time slots tested per cycle. Grows with `bf_window` and `bf_resolution`.
panels.title: Time-Slot Table Size

panels.description:
Rate of backfill cycles that ended for each reason — same data as `sdiag` → 'Backfilling stats' → 'Last cycle exited as'. The healthy series is `End of job queue`; every other reason means the cycle was cut short.

- `End of job queue` (`slurm_bf_exit_end`) — examined every pending job and finished. Desired.
- `Hit bf_max_job_start` (`slurm_bf_exit_max_job_start`) — started `bf_max_job_start` jobs in one cycle (default 0 = unlimited).
- `Hit bf_max_job_test` (`slurm_bf_exit_max_job_test`) — examined `bf_max_job_test` jobs and stopped (default 100). Raise to let backfill look deeper into the queue.
- `System state changed` (`slurm_bf_exit_state_changed`) — a job completed or a node state changed mid-cycle; backfill restarts with fresh data. Benign, but a sustained rate means the cluster is so dynamic that backfill never finishes a clean pass.
- `Hit table size limit (bf_node_space_size)` (`slurm_bf_exit_table_limit`) — backfill's per-node reservation table hit `bf_node_space_size` entries (default 50, capped at 4000); cycle stops to avoid runaway memory.
- `Timeout (bf_max_time)` (`slurm_bf_exit_timeout`) — cycle exceeded `bf_max_time` seconds (default 30, capped by `bf_interval`).

A non-zero rate on any reason other than `End of job queue` (or, sometimes, `System state changed`) means backfill is being throttled — raise the matching cap in `SchedulerParameters`.
panels.title: Backfill Exit Reasons

panels.description: Working-set memory of each container in the controller pod, stacked. The dashed line shows node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes — when the stacked total approaches it, the node is running out of allocatable memory.
panels.title: Memory Working Set

panels.description: Working-set memory as a percentage of the per-container memory limit. Containers with no limit set fall back to node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes (memory the kernel believes is allocatable without pressure) as the denominator.
panels.title: Memory Consumption %

panels.description: Active vs inactive memory on the controller node. Active is recently used; inactive is reclaimable on demand.

targets.legendFormat: Active - Memory that has been used more recently and usually not reclaimed unless absolutely necessary
Inactive - Memory which has been less recently used. It is more eligible to be reclaimed for other purposes

panels.title: Memory Active / Inactive

panels.description: Committed_AS vs CommitLimit. The kernel will refuse new allocations once Committed_AS approaches the limit (when overcommit is disabled).

matcher.options: CommitLimit - Amount of memory currently available to be allocated on the system

targets.legendFormat: Committed_AS - Amount of memory presently allocated on the system
CommitLimit - Amount of memory currently available to be allocated on the system

panels.title: Memory Committed

panels.description: Pages awaiting writeback to disk and dirty pages. Sustained high values can indicate slow storage or heavy IO.

targets.legendFormat: Writeback - Memory which is actively being written back to disk
WritebackTmp - Memory used by FUSE for temporary writeback buffers
Dirty - Memory which is waiting to get written back to the disk

panels.title: Memory Writeback and Dirty

panels.description: Minor and major page faults per second on the controller node. Major faults imply disk IO under memory pressure.

targets.legendFormat: Pgfault - Page major and minor fault operations

panels.title: Memory Page Faults

panels.description: Rate of OOM kill events on the controller node (node_vmstat_oom_kill).
panels.title: OOM Killer

panels.description: Rate of pages read from / written to disk by the kernel (node_vmstat_pgpgin/pgpgout). High values can correlate with memory pressure or heavy IO.
panels.title: Memory Pages In/Out

panels.description: Shared memory and file mappings on the controller node. slurmctld uses POSIX shared memory for IPC; large or growing values can hint at file-backed working set or IPC pressure.

targets.legendFormat: Mapped - Used memory in mapped pages files which have been mapped, such as libraries
Shmem - Used shared memory (shared between several processes, thus including RAM disks)
ShmemHugePages - Memory used by shared memory (shmem) and tmpfs allocated with huge pages
ShmemPmdMapped - Amount of shared (shmem/tmpfs) memory backed by huge pages

panels.title: Memory Shared and Mapped

panels.description: Anonymous (heap/stack) memory on the controller node. AnonPages is the closest single signal for slurmctld's actual memory footprint outside of file-backed caches.

targets.legendFormat: AnonHugePages - Memory in anonymous huge pages
AnonPages - Memory in user pages not backed by files

panels.title: Memory Anonymous

panels.description:
Container-level memory breakdown for the controller pod, split by container. Complements **Memory Working Set** with three orthogonal views of where the pod's memory is sitting:

- **`rss`** — actual heap + private anonymous mappings. Typically the dominant component for slurmctld.
- **`cache`** — kernel page cache touched by this container's file I/O. Reclaimable; not real pressure.
- **`mapped_file`** — pages mmap'd into the container's address space (subset of cache). slurmctld memory-maps state files in `StateSaveLocation`, so this can be MBs–GBs on busy clusters.

Sum of `rss + cache` ≈ container memory usage; sum of `rss + (cache − mapped_file)` is roughly what's accounted as Working Set.
panels.title: Container Memory Breakdown

panels.description: Network throughput on the controller pod's network namespace (rate over 5m). RX positive, TX negative.
panels.title: Network Throughput

panels.description: Per-NIC packet rate (rate over 5m) on the controller node. Rx as positive, Tx as negative for visual symmetry.
panels.title: Network Traffic by Packets

panels.description: Per-NIC packet errors and drops on the controller node. Both should be zero in healthy state.
panels.title: Network Errors & Drops

panels.description: Currently established TCP connections on the controller node. For slurmctld this directly reflects how many slurmd/srun/sacct clients are connected.

targets.legendFormat: CurrEstab - TCP connections in ESTABLISHED or CLOSE-WAIT

panels.title: TCP Connections

panels.description: TCP-level errors and retransmits. Listen drops and retransmits are early signs of overload or network problems.

targets.legendFormat: ListenDrops - SYNs to LISTEN sockets ignored (overflow)
OutRsts - segments sent with RST flag

panels.title: TCP Errors & Retransmits

panels.description: Netfilter connection-tracking entries vs limit. slurmctld sees many concurrent connections; if entries approach the limit, new connections fail silently.
panels.title: NF Conntrack

panels.description: Per-NIC operational state. UP/carrier should be 1 in healthy state — drops to 0 indicate the interface is administratively down or the cable/link has gone away.
panels.title: Network Operational Status

panels.description: Pod-level network throughput on controller-0 (RX = receive, TX = transmit). Source is the `node-exporter` sidecar running in the controller pod, scraped via `VMPodScrape` `custom-node-exporter`. Excludes `lo`. Note: this is **pod-level** traffic (slurmctld + sidecars), distinct from the host-level Network row below which shows the whole K8s node.
panels.title: Network Bandwidth

panels.description: Currently established TCP connections inside the controller pod (`node_netstat_Tcp_CurrEstab`). This is essentially the count of live slurmctld RPC clients plus a few sidecar/operator connections. Spikes correlate with bursts of `sbatch`/`squeue`/`sinfo` traffic.
panels.title: TCP Established Connections

panels.description: Pod-level packet rate (RX/TX packets per second), excluding `lo`. Useful for spotting packet-flood patterns that don't show up clearly in bytes (lots of small RPCs).
panels.title: Packet Rate

panels.description: Rate of TCP retransmissions (`node_netstat_Tcp_RetransSegs`) and SYN retransmissions (`node_netstat_TcpExt_TCPSynRetrans`). Both should sit near zero. Sustained non-zero values indicate packet loss between the pod and its peers — slurmd nodes, slurmdbd, or external clients.
panels.title: TCP Retransmits

panels.description: Interface-level errors and drops (`node_network_receive_errs_total`, `node_network_receive_drop_total`, `node_network_transmit_errs_total`, `node_network_transmit_drop_total`), excluding `lo`. Healthy is flat-zero. Anything non-zero typically means a network-stack or NIC problem.
panels.title: Network Errors & Drops

panels.description: Rate of TCP listen-queue overflows (`node_netstat_TcpExt_ListenDrops`) and connections from the SYN-cookie / SYN-queue path that got dropped. A non-zero rate here is a strong signal that slurmctld is being overwhelmed and the kernel can't keep up enqueuing new connections — cross-reference with the RPC row's *Outbound RPC* and inbound rate panels.
panels.title: TCP Listen Queue Drops

panels.description: CPU cores consumed by each container in the controller pod (rate over 5m).
panels.title: CPU Usage (cores)

panels.description: Per-mode CPU utilisation on the controller node, stacked to 100%. Iowait, Steal, and Softirq are particularly informative when the controller feels slow.

targets.legendFormat: System - Processes executing in kernel mode
User - Normal processes executing in user mode
Nice - Niced processes in user mode
Iowait - Waiting for I/O to complete
Steal - Time stolen by the hypervisor
Idle - Waiting for something to happen

panels.title: CPU Mode Breakdown

panels.description: System load averages over 1, 5 and 15 minutes. Includes runnable + uninterruptible-IO tasks; values trending above the CPU count indicate scheduling backlog.
panels.title: System Load

panels.description: Context-switch and interrupt rate on the controller node. Spikes here often correlate with bursts of slurmctld RPC traffic.
panels.title: Context Switches & Interrupts

panels.description:
Multi-dimensional pressure on the controller node:
• Kubelet MemoryPressure (binary 0/100) — when kubelet flags the node memory-pressured, it may evict pods.
• Memory PSI some/full — kernel fraction of time tasks were stalled waiting for memory.
• CPU PSI some — fraction of time tasks were stalled waiting for CPU.
• I/O PSI some/full — fraction of time tasks were stalled on I/O.

PSI usually rises before the kubelet condition trips.

targets.legendFormat: Memory PSI some - % time at least one task stalled on memory
Memory PSI full - % time ALL tasks stalled on memory
CPU PSI some - % time at least one task stalled on CPU
I/O PSI some - % time at least one task stalled on I/O
I/O PSI full - % time ALL tasks stalled on I/O

panels.title: Pressure

list.description: Controller pod to inspect. Discovered from kube_pod_info; defaults to controller-0.
list.label: Controller Pod
list.name: pod

list.description: K8s node hosting the selected controller pod. Hidden; used for joining node-exporter metrics.
list.label: Node
list.name: node

list.description: node-exporter `instance` label for the controller node. Hidden; used to filter node_* metrics.
list.label: Instance
list.name: instance
